content.problemStatement:
Krillin has died yet again. Goku and Picollo must find a dragon ball in order to resurrect him. Kami gives them a quiz to solve in return for the dragon ball.

He gives them an array AAA (having 1 based indexing) of NNN integers and he'll ask them QQQ queries. The array is basically a frequency table which represents some data. It signifies that iii occurs A[i]A[i]A[i] times in the data. The queries can be of two types -

1 LLL RRR

2 iii xxx

First query - They must find the modemodemode and medianmedianmedian in the range LLL to RRR where LLL and RRR are inclusive (LLL and RRR are indices of the array) and return their LCMLCMLCM. It is guaranteed that there will be an unique mode.

Second query- The element at iiith index must be updated to xxx.

111 represents the first query and 222 represents the second query.

Note: Use greatest integer function for computing the median. This means that the median should be the floor value if the computed median is a fraction.

###Input:


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN, QQQ separated by a single space.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers denoting elements of the array separated by single spaces.
The next QQQ lines contain respective queries.
###Output:
For each test case, print results of only the first type of queries in separate lines.

###Constraints


1≤T≤21 \leq T \leq 21≤T≤2
1≤N≤1051 \leq N \leq 10^51≤N≤105
1≤Q≤1051 \leq Q \leq 10^51≤Q≤105
1≤L≤R≤N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1≤L≤R≤N
1≤x≤1091 \leq x \leq 10^91≤x≤109
1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N
1≤A1,A2,A3,...,An≤1091 \leq A_1, A_2, A_3, ..., A_n \leq 10^91≤A1​,A2​,A3​,...,An​≤109
At every point of time, the frequency array will have all distinct elements. That is, at any particular moment, there will never be Ai=AjA_i = A_jAi​=Aj​.
###Sample Input:
2
5 3
2 4 6 8 9

1 1 3

2 2 56

1 1 3

7 6

20 8 50 16 22 23 29

1 1 6

2 2 18

1 1 4

2 6 10

2 5 11

1 5 6

###Sample Output:
6
2

3

3

5

###EXPLANATION:
Example case 1: The frequency array is 2 4 6 8 9. For the first query- 1 1 3 the range is 1 to 3, i.e. for frequency 2 to 6. The mode element is 3 since it has the maximum frequency(6) in this subarray and the median is 2.5 which will be floored down to 2. Their Lcm is 6. So for this query 6 will be the output.

For the second query - 2 2 56 The element at the 2nd index which is 4 will be replaced by 56. The array will now become 2 56 6 8 9. This array will be used for the upcoming queries. But for a query of this type, no output is there.

For the third query- 1 1 3 The array is 2 56 6 8 9 and the range is 1 to 3 with corresponding frequencies 2, 56, 6. The mode element is 2 since it has the maximum frequency(56) in this subarray and the median is 2. Their LCM i.e 2 is the output.
constraints: At every point of time, the frequency array will have all distinct elements.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and Q, where N is the number of elements in the array and Q is the number of queries. The next line contains N integers representing the array elements. The following Q lines contain queries, each starting with an integer (1 or 2) indicating the type of query. For type 1 queries, the next two integers L and R represent the range of indices to consider. For type 2 queries, the next two integers i and x represent the index to update and the new value, respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each type 1 query, output the LCM of the mode and median of the elements in the specified range.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first query, the mode is 3 and the median is 2, so the LCM is 6. For the second query, the value at index 2 is updated to 56. For the third query, the mode is 2 and the median is 2, so the LCM is 2.

For the first query, the mode is 3 and the median is 3, so the LCM is 3. For the second query, the value at index 2 is updated to 18. For the third query, the mode is 3 and the median is 3, so the LCM is 3. For the fourth query, the value at index 6 is updated to 10. For the fifth query, the value at index 5 is updated to 11. For the sixth query, the mode is 5 and the median is 5, so the LCM is 5.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we first need to understand the concept of mode, median, and LCM. The mode is the value that appears most frequently in the array, the median is the middle value when the array is sorted, and the LCM is the least common multiple of two numbers. For each type 1 query, we need to find the mode and median of the elements in the specified range and then calculate their LCM. For type 2 queries, we need to update the value at the specified index.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q * N) — because for each query, we are potentially scanning the entire array to find the mode and median.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we need to store the array of size N.
